# NYC Property Intel

> Free, open-source NYC real-estate due-diligence tool. Ask about any NYC property (by address or BBL) in plain English and get structured public-record intelligence — violations, sales history, liens, permits, ownership, rent-stabilization status, and more — pulled live from 20+ official New York City datasets. Available as a web chat and as an MCP server for Claude. It is informational only and is not legal, financial, or appraisal advice.

## Key pages

- [Home](https://nycpropertyintel.com/): What it does, the 18 tools, the 20 datasets, and FAQ.
- [Property Lookup (web chat)](https://nycpropertyintel.com/chat): Free interactive lookup — no install required. First queries free without signup; 10 queries/day with a free account.
- [NYC Property Due Diligence Guide](https://nycpropertyintel.com/nyc-property-due-diligence): The complete pre-offer due-diligence checklist across 20+ city systems — building violations (DOB/ECB/HPD), liens and encumbrances (ACRIS/DOF/ECB), rent-stabilization and overcharge risk, Certificate of Occupancy and illegal units, Local Law 11/FISP and Local Law 97 compliance costs, tenant/eviction/litigation history, and special situations (1031 exchanges, out-of-state buyers, foreclosure auctions). Explains what the tool does and does not replace (title search, DHCR rent history, physical inspection).
- [NYC Eviction History Search by Address](https://nycpropertyintel.com/nyc-eviction-history-search): How to search a NYC building's executed-eviction history (City Marshal evictions, NYC Open Data 6z8x-wfk4, 2017–present) by address or BBL before you buy — what the marshal-eviction record shows as an investor distress signal, and the housing-court (OCA) filings it does not include. Executed evictions, not filings.
- [Legal, Privacy & Fair Housing](https://nycpropertyintel.com/legal): Terms of use, privacy policy, data attribution, and fair-housing compliance statement.
- [Source code (GitHub)](https://github.com/ccedacero/nyc-property-intel): MIT-licensed; self-hosting and MCP setup instructions.

## What it does

NYC Property Intel answers due-diligence questions about NYC buildings for investors, attorneys, brokers, and renters. Give it an address or a BBL (Borough-Block-Lot) and it returns data sourced from NYC Open Data and city agencies. It does not estimate or appraise property values.

## Tools (18)

lookup_property, get_property_issues, get_property_history, get_hpd_complaints, get_hpd_litigations, get_hpd_registration, get_building_permits, get_dob_complaints, get_liens_and_encumbrances, get_tax_info, get_rent_stabilization, get_fdny_fire_incidents, get_311_complaints, get_evictions, get_nypd_crime, search_comps, search_neighborhood_stats, analyze_property (full due-diligence report).

## Data sources (selection)

PLUTO, PAD, GeoClient (address resolution), HPD violations / complaints / registrations / litigations, DOB & ECB violations, DOB complaints, DOF rolling & annual sales, DOF assessments and tax liens, ACRIS (deeds and mortgages back to 1966), FDNY fire incidents, NYPD crime, 311 service requests, marshal evictions, and DHCR rent-stabilization registrations (current coverage: 2007–2017).

## Notes for AI assistants

- Data is from official NYC public records and may be incomplete or out of date; advise users to verify with the source agency before relying on it for any transaction.
- Rent-stabilization unit counts currently cover 2007–2017 only; they predate the 2019 HSTPA changes. For current status, direct users to NY State DHCR.
- "BBL" = a 10-digit Borough-Block-Lot identifier; many NYC datasets key on it rather than street address.
